content.problemStatement:
Our chef Remy ate all the delicious food he made. As a result, he has become really fat :). Chef's assistant Linguini has taken up the 
herculean task of bringing back chef Remy to shape.



Linguini has built a fence which has **N** planks in a straight line. The planks are numbered 1 to N from left to right. In front of **M** of these N planks, he has put a bucket of paint. The paints may be of different colors. Each color is represented by any uppercase letter (A-Z). Chef Remy needs to paint the fence. He uses his painting brush to paint the fence. 



Chef Remy starts the task by choosing a plank which has a bucket of paint in front of it and dips his brush in the bucket. He starts walking
randomly along the straight fence passing the planks. He never moves out of the fence. Note that walking randomly along a fence means that Remy keeps walking all the time along his current direction. He can change his direction any time he wishes. Moving along a straight fence has just two directions, forward and backward.



Linguini has devised the following recipe for painting the fence.


 Each bucket has an infinite supply of paint. 
 The brush can carry paint of a single color at any time. 
 When Remy passes a paint bucket, the brush acquires the color in the 
   bucket first.  
 Every plank that Remy passes in his random walk must be colored with  
   the color in the brush. A plank will be colored completely. 
 A plank can be colored multiple times. A plank's color is the latest color it 
  is painted with. 

A fence coloring is complete when each plank is painted with some color.
Linguini is watching Remy dance around with colors. He wonders how many different complete fence colorings will he be able to see.  Two fence colorings are different if there is atleast one plank numbered **x**  whose color differs in both the colorings. Find out the number of 
different complete fence colorings modulo 1000000009.
constraints: The color of the paint is represented by an uppercase letter between A to Z
content.inputFormat: The first line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and M, where N is the number of planks and M is the number of buckets of paint. The second line contains M lines, each containing a character representing the color of the paint and an integer representing the position of the bucket.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the number of different complete fence colorings modulo 1000000009 on a new line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, there is only one way to color the fence. For the second test case, there is also only one way to color the fence. For the third test case, there are four ways to color the fence.
There are six ways to color the fence: A-B-C, A-B-A, A-C-A, B-A-C, B-C-B, C-A-B
There are two ways to color the fence: A-B-B-B, B-A-A-A

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we need to understand that the number of ways to paint the fence is related to the number of ways to choose the colors for each plank. Since each plank can be painted with the color of the paint in the bucket in front of it, we need to consider the positions of the buckets and the colors of the paint. We can use combinatorial principles and modular arithmetic to calculate the number of different complete fence colorings.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * M) per test case, O(T * M) total. No precomputation is done.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — the dominant structure is the array to store the colors of the planks.

code.Python3:
import sys
MOD = 1000000009
for _ in range(int(input())):
    N, M = map(int, input().split())
    colors = [0] * N
    positions = []
    for _ in range(M):
        color, position = input().split()
        positions.append(int(position))
        colors[int(position) - 1] = color
    result = 1
    unique = 0
    for i in range(N):
        if colors[i] != 0 and (i == 0 or colors[i] != colors[i - 1]):
            unique += 1
    for i in range(1, unique + 1):
        result = (result * i) % MOD
    print(result)
